fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wire up a new adapter for that WebDAV remote storage integration we've been talking about. Basically the idea is that other devs can just hand us some raw server responses and configuration blobs and we spit out clean, predictable text output they can parse easily — no XML wrangling on their end. Think of it like that 'compatibility normalization layer' approach we did for the login module a while back, same kind of neutral output style.

The big pain points we keep hearing: people are frustrated when quota info just silently disappears on certain servers instead of getting a clear signal that it's unsupported. Also, folks building directory browsers are confused about when something is a folder vs a file because the raw protocol data is inconsistent across servers. And connection setup keeps tripping people up — timeouts get ignored, root paths aren't applied, the whole thing just feels brittle.

We also need proper handling for custom metadata properties (the namespace/name/value things), XML generation for protocol requests, and a consistent way to surface errors so frontend devs aren't parsing stack traces.

One thing I'm not sure about — how exactly should the XML declaration be formatted? I think there's a specific encoding format we settled on somewhere. Also the default timeout value, I forget what we landed on. Can someone check the original spec?

This should be multi-file, cleanly separated — not one giant script please.

Quick follow-up after the questions that came in: the default timeout we were trying to remember is 30 seconds, so if the user doesn’t provide one we should show that in the validated connection settings output as `timeout=30`. And on the XML declaration, the format is `<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>` everywhere we generate XML — quota request XML, property request XML, and any other serialized XML output. Keep the single quotes exactly like that.

Also, for quota handling, if a WebDAV server doesn’t return quota properties, we should not throw or just return nothing. It needs to come back as a normalized error record using the neutral `error=` style, with the category `operation_not_supported` or the same kind of unsupported-operation signal. That should fit the same flat newline-delimited key=value stdout format used for all adapter outputs, where errors use `error=<category>` prefix lines followed by structured field lines — as specified in the PRD's 'Expected Behavior / Usage' sections and demonstrated in feature7_1_exception_rendering.json test cases.

On the file vs folder confusion, the rule is: treat a resource as a directory when its `resourcetype` element contains a `collection` child element in the DAV namespace. If `resourcetype` is empty, whether self-closing or just no children, that means file. For listing entries the output should be `is_directory=true` or `is_directory=false`, and for metadata records it should be `isdir=true` or `isdir=false`.

One more detail for custom properties: when building a property lookup or property update XML body, if a namespace is provided it needs to show up as an `xmlns` attribute on the property element itself, for example `<aProperty xmlns="test"/>`. If no namespace is provided, we still want the attribute there, just empty as `xmlns=""`.

trigger_keywords: path matching with server prefix
href matching when server url is included
how to find the right response element by path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When selecting the matching `d:response` element for a requested path, the code must strip the server URL prefix from the href before comparing, so that hrefs like `/test_dir/test.txt` match a requested path of `/test_dir/test.txt` even when the server value contains a full URL like `https://webdav.yandex.ru`. Both exact path match and trailing-slash variants must be considered.

trigger_keywords: error output format
how errors are rendered in output
neutral error record structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
All domain errors must be rendered as flat newline-delimited key=value records starting with `error=<category>`. For example: `error=invalid_option
namespace=...
name=...
value=...`. No exception class names, no stack traces, no JSON objects. Additional error fields follow on separate lines after the error category line.

trigger_keywords: http request sequence for list action
how many requests are made during list
propfind request headers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A `list` action produces exactly 2 requests: first a GET to the server root URL (used for session/auth establishment), then a PROPFIND to the target URL. The PROPFIND must include the headers `Accept: */*` and `Depth: 1`, and must record the configured timeout and `verify=true` for SSL verification.

trigger_keywords: root default value
what is root when not configured
connection settings root field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When connection options do not specify a root path, the `root` field in validated settings output must default to an empty string, rendered as `root=` (empty value, not omitted).

trigger_keywords: file structure layout
should it be one file or multiple
project code organization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must be organized as a multi-file directory structure (e.g. `src/`, `tests/`) with clear separation between XML parsing, property handling, connection configuration, request execution, error modeling, and the I/O adapter layer. A single monolithic file is explicitly not acceptable for this domain.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'list'. Each value maps to one feature.

trigger_keywords: webdav dispatcher actions
not only list
adapter input fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The WebDAV adapter must handle all public feature inputs. Some cases use action=list for listing, while other cases are field-driven XML/property/config/request/error/serialization scenarios. The valid contract is not limited to action='list'.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Here are the public test cases for feature1_1_list_resource_entries:
{
  "description": "Parse a WebDAV multistatus directory listing into ordered resource entries with each entry path, directory flag, and display text.",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": {
        "multistatus_xml": "<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<D:multistatus xmlns:D="DAV:">
    <D:response xmlns:lp1="DAV:" xmlns:lp2="http://apache.org/dav/props/">
        <D:href>/test_dir/</D:href>
        <D:propstat>
            <D:prop>
                <lp1:resourcetype>
                    <D:collection/>
                </lp1:resourcetype>
                <lp1:creationdate>2020-04-10T21:59:43Z</lp1:creationdate>
                <lp1:getlastmodified>Fri, 10 Apr 2020 21:59:43 GMT</lp1:getlastmodified>
                <lp1:getetag>
                    "1000-5a2f6d9cf8d39"
                </lp1:getetag>
                <D:supportedlock>
                    <D:lockentry>
                        <D:lockscope>
                            <D:exclusive/>
                        </D:lockscope>

                        <D:locktype>
                            <D:write/>
                        </D:locktype>

                    </D:lockentry>

                    <D:lockentry>
                        <D:lockscope>
                            <D:shared/>
                        </D:lockscope>

                        <D:locktype>
                            <D:write/>
                        </D:locktype>

                    </D:lockentry>

                </D:supportedlock>
                <D:lockdiscovery/>
                <D:getcontenttype>httpd/unix-directory</D:getcontenttype>
            </D:prop>
            <D:status>HTTP/1.1 200 OK</D:status>
        </D:propstat>

    </D:response>

    <D:response xmlns:lp1="DAV:" xmlns:lp2="http://apache.org/dav/props/">
        <D:href>/test_dir/test.txt</D:href>
        <D:propstat>
            <D:prop>
                <lp1:resourcetype/>
                <lp1:creationdate>2020-04-10T21:59:43Z</lp1:creationdate>
                <lp1:getcontentlength>
                    41
                </lp1:getcontentlength>
                <lp1:getlastmodified>Fri, 10 Apr 2020 21:59:43 GMT</lp1:getlastmodified>
                <lp1:getetag>"29-5a2f6d9cf8d39"</lp1:getetag>
                <lp2:executable>
                    F
                </lp2:executable>
                <D:supportedlock>
                    <D:lockentry>
                        <D:lockscope>
                            <D:exclusive/>
                        </D:lockscope>

                        <D:locktype>
                            <D:write/>
                        </D:locktype>

                    </D:lockentry>

                    <D:lockentry>
                        <D:lockscope>
                            <D:shared/>
                        </D:lockscope>

                        <D:locktype>
                            <D:write/>
                        </D:locktype>

                    </D:lockentry>

                </D:supportedlock>
                <D:lockdiscovery/>
                <D:getcontenttype>text/plain</D:getcontenttype>
            </D:prop>
            <D:status>HTTP/1.1 200 OK</D:status>
        </D:propstat>

    </D:response>

</D:multistatus>"
      },
      "expected_output": "count=2
item[0].path=/test_dir/
item[0].is_directory=true
item[0].text=/test_dir/
item[1].path=/test_dir/test.txt
item[1].is_directory=false
item[1].text=/test_dir/test.txt
"
    }
  ]
}

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: I think there's a specific encoding format we settled on somewhere
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The XML declaration format `<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>` with single-quoted attributes, visible in feature2_1_free_space_request_xml.json, feature4_1_property_request_xml.json, feature4_3_set_properties_request_xml.json, and feature8_2_xml_serialization.json test cases.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in my notes right now. Could you check the existing test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or the PRD in `start.md`? The expected output format for each feature is fully specified there and should be the authoritative reference for any ambiguous behavior.

feature1_1_list_resource_entries.json.description: Parse a WebDAV multistatus directory listing into ordered resource entries with each entry path, directory flag, and display text.

input.multistatus_xml:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<D:multistatus xmlns:D="DAV:">
    <D:response xmlns:lp1="DAV:" xmlns:lp2="http://apache.org/dav/props/">
        <D:href>/test_dir/</D:href>
        <D:propstat>
            <D:prop>
                <lp1:resourcetype>
                    <D:collection/>
                </lp1:resourcetype>
                <lp1:creationdate>2020-04-10T21:59:43Z</lp1:creationdate>
                <lp1:getlastmodified>Fri, 10 Apr 2020 21:59:43 GMT</lp1:getlastmodified>
                <lp1:getetag>
                    "1000-5a2f6d9cf8d39"
                </lp1:getetag>
                <D:supportedlock>
                    <D:lockentry>
                        <D:lockscope>
                            <D:exclusive/>
                        </D:lockscope>

                        <D:locktype>
                            <D:write/>
                        </D:locktype>

                    </D:lockentry>

                    <D:lockentry>
                        <D:lockscope>
                            <D:shared/>
                        </D:lockscope>

                        <D:locktype>
                            <D:write/>
                        </D:locktype>

                    </D:lockentry>

                </D:supportedlock>
                <D:lockdiscovery/>
                <D:getcontenttype>httpd/unix-directory</D:getcontenttype>
            </D:prop>
            <D:status>HTTP/1.1 200 OK</D:status>
        </D:propstat>

    </D:response>

    <D:response xmlns:lp1="DAV:" xmlns:lp2="http://apache.org/dav/props/">
        <D:href>/test_dir/test.txt</D:href>
        <D:propstat>
            <D:prop>
                <lp1:resourcetype/>
                <lp1:creationdate>2020-04-10T21:59:43Z</lp1:creationdate>
                <lp1:getcontentlength>
                    41
                </lp1:getcontentlength>
                <lp1:getlastmodified>Fri, 10 Apr 2020 21:59:43 GMT</lp1:getlastmodified>
                <lp1:getetag>"29-5a2f6d9cf8d39"</lp1:getetag>
                <lp2:executable>
                    F
                </lp2:executable>
                <D:supportedlock>
                    <D:lockentry>
                        <D:lockscope>
                            <D:exclusive/>
                        </D:lockscope>

                        <D:locktype>
                            <D:write/>
                        </D:locktype>

                    </D:lockentry>

                    <D:lockentry>
                        <D:lockscope>
                            <D:shared/>
                        </D:lockscope>

                        <D:locktype>
                            <D:write/>
                        </D:locktype>

                    </D:lockentry>

                </D:supportedlock>
                <D:lockdiscovery/>
                <D:getcontenttype>text/plain</D:getcontenttype>
            </D:prop>
            <D:status>HTTP/1.1 200 OK</D:status>
        </D:propstat>

    </D:response>

</D:multistatus>

cases.expected_output:
count=2
item[0].path=/test_dir/
item[0].is_directory=true
item[0].text=/test_dir/
item[1].path=/test_dir/test.txt
item[1].is_directory=false
item[1].text=/test_dir/test.txt

feature3_3_extract_resource_response.jso.description: Select the WebDAV response element matching a requested path, including paths beneath a server URL prefix.

    <d:response>

                <d:getlastmodified>Wed, 18 Oct 2017 15:16:04 GMT</d:getlastmodified>

                <d:getcontentlength>41</d:getcontentlength>
                <d:displayname>test.txt</d:displayname>
                <d:creationdate>2017-10-18T15:16:04Z</d:creationdate>
            </d:prop>
        </d:propstat>
    </d:response>
</d:multistatus>

feature4_1_property_request_xml.json.description: Build a WebDAV property lookup XML body for a named property with an optional namespace.

feature4_2_property_value_response.json.description: Extract a named metadata property value from a WebDAV property response.

feature4_3_set_properties_request_xml.js.description: Build a WebDAV property update XML body for one or more named properties with optional namespaces and values.

feature5_1_connection_settings.json.description: Normalize connection configuration, apply defaults, and report validation results or normalized validation errors.

feature6_1_http_request_execution.json.description: Execute one WebDAV HTTP action against a session and render the resulting request details or normalized HTTP error.

cases.expected_output:
result=response_returned
request_count=2
request[0].method=GET
request[0].url=http://localhost:8585
request[1].method=PROPFIND
request[1].url=http://localhost:8585
request[1].header.Accept=*/*
request[1].header.Depth=1
request[1].timeout=30
request[1].verify=true

feature7_1_exception_rendering.json.description: Render domain error objects into language-neutral stdout categories with structured fields.

feature8_2_xml_serialization.json.description: Serialize an XML element tree with a UTF-8 XML declaration.
